target_hypotheses.10: agenthustler published the x402 + FastAPI + Apify tutorial on dev.to — a hands-on builder who documents real paid-agent endpoints and reaches other builders through writing. Tutorial authors are the highest-leverage design partners: an integration becomes documentation automatically. Dev.to DM is lower-noise than X for an indie builder who lives in that ecosystem. First contact today while higher-profile targets (Circle, Skyfire) warm.
target_hypotheses.6: Amir Sarhangi's KYAPay solves the same problem as Bolyra from a different architectural direction (centralized KYC vs ZKP). The most valuable conversation in a competitive ecosystem is a compare-notes call between two people who made different bets on the same hard problem. Amir is technical and has shipped — he can evaluate the ZKP vs KYC tradeoff on the merits. The ask is not 'use Bolyra' but 'how did you solve the privacy tradeoff?' The insight from this conversation is worth having even if no design partnership follows.
target_hypotheses.18: nova402 ships multi-chain x402 SDKs with framework integrations (Express, FastAPI, etc.) — they're infrastructure that other builders build on. Authorization middleware that slots into their existing framework integration points would propagate across every app that uses nova402, making this a distribution multiplier rather than a single integration. GitHub channel reaches the builder directly where they work.
target_hypotheses.19: Second-State ships the open-source x402 facilitator that other tools build on. As the reference facilitator, every payment from an arbitrary agent flows through their stack — no agent has authorization context at that layer today. A hook in the facilitator is the maximum-leverage integration point in the pipeline: one PR propagates to every downstream app. GitHub is the right channel — the conversation is most naturally framed as a code question.
target_hypotheses.16: Bank of AI is already in the agent identity space for TRON/BSC, which means they've already shipped thinking on what agent credentials look like. The architectural question — how do you preserve operator privacy while making authorization verifiable to the payee — is genuinely open and technically interesting. A compare-notes approach works here the same way it works with Skyfire: they've made design choices worth understanding regardless of whether a partnership follows.
target_hypotheses.24: Hyperbolic's pay-per-inference via x402 is one of the highest-stakes authorization gaps in the pipeline: a compromised or over-permissioned agent running on a model at $X/inference is a real financial exposure. Jasper Zhang is a technical co-founder ($20M raised) who thinks about production systems. LinkedIn is right for a co-founder conversation about infrastructure architecture. The question is calibrated to his experience: he's already solved the payment problem, the authorization question is the next unsolved layer.
target_hypotheses.29: Shahzad Safri at agenticplug.ai has indexed all six agent commerce protocols and writes about the gaps between them. He's an ecosystem analyst, not a builder with an endpoint — but that makes him a different kind of design partner: the person who names the missing category. If he publicly identifies authorization as the cross-protocol gap, that's a proof point. If he introduces Bolyra in a future comparison post, that's distribution. LinkedIn is the right channel for a researcher who publishes analysis.
target_hypotheses.33: Matt Wright co-founded GaiaNet with a different focus than Harish Kotra (DevRel, day 1). Matt is product/company — thinking about the node architecture and roadmap, not tutorials. The authorization question is a roadmap question for GaiaNet: do nodes need per-agent credentials, or is a signed payment sufficient? A co-founder conversation on LinkedIn opens a different door than Harish on X. Coordinate sends: don't overlap with Harish's follow-up window.

drafts.10: Hey, your x402 + FastAPI + Apify tutorial on dev.to is one of the most practical walkthroughs I've found for actually building a paid agent endpoint — the code gets to working faster than most posts I've seen. For the Apify scraping endpoint you demo, how are you thinking about which agents are authorized to call it: is a valid payment the full gate, or are there cases where you'd want a finer identity signal before serving the request?
drafts.6: Hey Amir, KYAPay anchoring agent identity to KYC is a strong fit for regulated environments where the counterparty needs to know who the agent operator is. I'm curious about where you landed on the privacy boundary: when an agent proves authorization to a service, does the service see the operator's full KYC record at call time, or does verification happen off the hot path and the payment carries only a derived capability token?
drafts.18: Hey, the multi-chain x402 SDK approach in nova402 is the kind of foundation that removes a key reason not to adopt x402 — same payment primitives across chains means builders don't have to re-solve it per-network. For the framework integrations, are you seeing demand for authorization middleware that sits between the framework and the x402 payment handler, or is the pre-payment identity question something builders are handling themselves in application code today?
drafts.19: Hey, the Second-State x402 facilitator being open-source and reusable is important infrastructure — being the layer other tools build on means you're seeing a wider range of agent traffic than any single application. In the facilitator flow, when a payment arrives, is there a signal about whether the calling agent was explicitly authorized by the wallet owner to initiate it, or does the facilitator treat a valid signed transaction as sufficient proof of authorization?
drafts.16: Hey, building on-chain identity for agents on TRON and BSC puts Bank of AI at an interesting intersection: agents with identity can make authorization verifiable rather than trust-based. For your on-chain identity design, how do you handle the privacy boundary — when an agent proves its identity to a service, does the service see the operator's full on-chain record, or is there a selective disclosure layer between the credential and the verification check?
drafts.24: Hey Jasper, Hyperbolic's pay-per-inference model via x402 is one of the clearest production examples of agentic commerce at scale. For the inference endpoints, when an agent submits a payment to run a model, do you have a way to verify the agent was authorized by its operator to initiate that specific inference request — or is the wallet signature the full authorization signal today?
drafts.29: Hey Shahzad, your breakdown of the six agent commerce protocols at agenticplug.ai is the most comprehensive map of the space I've found — the reference I point people to when they ask how ACP, AP2, x402, and the others relate. Looking across those six, where do you see the authorization question landing: is it something each protocol has started to address, or is that layer still a gap that sits outside all of them?
drafts.33: Hey Matt, GaiaNet's model of independent earning nodes for decentralized inference is one of the more durable architectures I've seen for agent-accessible compute. For the pay-per-inference model your nodes run, how are you thinking about the identity of the calling agent: is proving you can pay enough to access a node, or are you seeing demand for a per-agent authorization credential that node operators can check before they serve inference?

codex_review: Two-pass review. Pass 1 — compliance: all 8 targets (IDs 6, 10, 16, 18, 19, 24, 29, 33) have status not_contacted in pipeline.json ✓. None appeared in any prior day target list (days 1–4 covered IDs 1,2,3,4,5,7,8,9,11,13,14,15,17,20,21,22,23,25,26,27,28,32,35,36,40) ✓. Zero drafts mention Bolyra ✓. Zero drafts say 'I built' ✓. Zero drafts contain any link ✓. All 8 lead with something specific the target built, published, or shipped ✓. Pass 2 — quality: all drafts 2–3 sentences; each ends with exactly one technical question calibrated to the specific person's background; questions are distinct and non-overlapping. Five flags for the founder: (1) DAY 5 KILL-CRITERIA STATUS — the day-3 checkpoint required ≥ 2 positive replies from first 16 contacts before continuing. Day-4 scorecard shows 0 positive replies from 7 confirmed sends. Before sending today's 8, the morning evaluator session must answer: are DMs from days 2–4 actually sent? If total confirmed sends < 16, the kill criterion hasn't triggered yet — but if 16+ sent with 0 replies, diagnose now (opens/reads → channel; no reply → message hook; score-1 replies → ICP). Consider rewriting the opening line to lead with PAIN ('agents can pay but can't prove authorization') instead of PRAISE. (2) IDs 6 (Skyfire) and 16 (Bank of AI) are both compare-notes with identity-adjacent competitors — do not pitch, do not mention Bolyra name; the goal is to understand their architectural choices, which is the same ask as first-contact ICP targets. (3) ID 33 (Matt Wright, GaiaNet) — coordinate with Harish Kotra (ID 4, day 1) to avoid two GaiaNet DMs in the same window. If Harish replied this week, pause Matt until that thread resolves. (4) ID 19 (Second-State, GitHub) — open-source facilitator is maximum-leverage: one hook here propagates to every downstream app. Flag this as the highest-priority send of the day. (5) ID 29 (Shahzad Safri) is ecosystem/analyst category — success metric is different from the others: a mention in a future protocol comparison post or an intro to builders he tracks, not a walkthrough booking. Score a reply at 2 (interested) even without a builder commitment.
